draft.introduction: At Gorgias 521d–e, Socrates claims to be the only Athenian who practices the true art of politics. While others flatter the demos, he alone aims at what is genuinely best for the city’s soul, even at the cost of his own life.

translation.text: I think I am one of the few Athenians, not to say the only one, who practices the true art of politics, and the only man of the present time who manages affairs of state. Since the speeches I make on each occasion aim not at gratification but at what is best, not at what is most pleasant, I would have nothing to say in my defense.
translation.original_text: οἶμαι μετ᾽ ὀλίγων Ἀθηναίων, ἵνα μὴ εἴπω μόνος, ἐπιχειρεῖν τῇ ὡς ἀληθῶς πολιτικῇ τέχνῃ

translator_notes.note: Socrates’s claim to practice “true politics” (ἀληθῶς πολιτικὴ τέχνη) redefines political action as care for the soul rather than the management of power. This redefinition became central to the Platonic philosophical tradition’s critique of conventional politics.
